 {"id":"ft-26k3h","title":"[MEDIUM] survival::ActivityProfile::update_hour NaN-poisons hourly_ewma via clamp","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/survival.rs:455` — `ActivityProfile::update_hour`.

Same `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` gotcha as ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5 / ft-x50ce. A NaN `normalized_activity`:

1. `sample = normalized_activity.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` stays NaN.
2. First sample (sample_count == 0) path writes `self.hourly_ewma[index] = NaN`.
3. Any subsequent EWMA update is `alpha * NaN + (1 - alpha) * prev = NaN`.
4. Every `predict_hour` / `predict` call returns NaN for that hour slot, forever.
5. Downstream survival/hazard math using the predicted activity gets NaN — same silent-wrong-answer class as disk_pressure.

## Fix

Guard at entry: if `normalized_activity.is_nan()`, skip the update entirely. The slot keeps its last good value (or the default if uninitialised) and the next well-formed sample continues the EWMA.

## Tests

* `ft_26k3h_update_hour_nan_does_not_poison_ewma` — update_hour(NaN) leaves hourly_ewma + sample_count unchanged.
* `ft_26k3h_update_hour_nan_then_clean_sample_still_tracks` — end-to-end: NaN → clean sample sequence still produces a finite, sensible EWMA value.

## Scope

Single file (`survival.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:50:37.797018Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:53:29.312035Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:53:29.311735Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-2funa","title":"[no-mocks-test] crates/frankenterm-core/tests/watchdog_labruntime.rs: pane-watchdog uses MockWezterm + FailingMockWezterm — should drive a real wezterm subprocess for failure injection","description":"## Mock at issue
crates/frankenterm-core/tests/watchdog_labruntime.rs:
  29: struct FailingMockWezterm;
  31: impl WeztermInterface for FailingMockWezterm
  161-167: mock_wezterm_handle() / mock_wezterm_handle_failing() helpers
The watchdog tests (179, 206, 235, ...) all run against these mocks.

## Why the mock is risky
The watchdog detects pane-death and triggers recovery. Production failure
modes the FailingMockWezterm cannot model:
- Real EAGAIN/EPIPE from a wezterm-mux socket
- Pane that returns successfully to list_panes() but is hung mid-write
- Concurrent panes where ONLY one is dead (mock fails uniformly)
- Slow pane (latency > timeout) vs dead pane — the watchdog must
  distinguish; FailingMockWezterm collapses both to "Err"
- Reconnect race after subprocess restart

Production incidents where the watchdog escalated wrongly (or didn't
escalate at all) would not be reproduced by the current FailingMock.

Mock Risk score: 4 (uptime: pane death undetected) × 4 (real flake
patterns are richer than the binary mock) = 16 → MUST be mock-free.

## Real fixture available
WeztermClient (src/wezterm.rs:859) is the production client. With a
wezterm-mux subprocess we can:
- kill -SIGSTOP the subprocess to simulate the hung pane
- close the unix socket from the mux side to simulate EPIPE
- spawn N panes and selectively kill subset to test partial failure

## Suggested approach
1. Add `WeztermFaultInjectingFixture` (extends the
   WeztermSubprocessFixture from ft-dvgzi) with primitives:
   .stop_pane(id), .kill_pane(id), .pause_socket(), .resume_socket(),
   .latency_inject(pane, duration).
2. Migrate ≥3 watchdog tests to use real wezterm + fault injection.
3. Keep MockWezterm for the pure-routing tests (state machine
   transitions that don't depend on actual pane I/O).
4. Add regression test for the partial-failure case: 4 panes, kill
   panes 1+3, verify watchdog escalates only those, leaves 0+2
   alone.
5. JSON-line logging: phase=fault_inject|escalate|recover with
   pane_id, error_kind, ms-from-injection.

## Acceptance
- ≥3 watchdog tests use real wezterm subprocess + fault injection.
- Partial-failure regression test passes.
- No flake under repeated runs.
- Existing MockWezterm tests still green.

## Depends on
- ft-dvgzi (snapshot_e2e WeztermSubprocessFixture) for the harness.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_2","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:11:56.078672Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T09:16:32.828234Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T09:16:32.827989Z","close_reason":"5 no-mocks watchdog tests landed in tests/watchdog_real_mux.rs (1950fb712). Successful-path tests (1, 3, 4) plus 2 REAL fault-injection tests (kill mux subprocess via fixture.kill_mux(), strict-socket guard ensures real CommandFailed propagation). All 5 pass under FT_REAL_WEZTERM_TESTS=1. Added .handle() + .kill_mux() helpers to WeztermSubprocessFixture. Tests 7/8 from the original labruntime suite (set_watchdog_warnings / set_watchdog_warning_error) are NOT migrated by design: they depend on the mock's external warning-injection channel that has no real-mux equivalent — real WeztermClient.watchdog_warnings derives from circuit-breaker state. The labruntime suite stays green for those mock-only paths; the new suite runs alongside under the FT_REAL_WEZTERM_TESTS gate.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["no-mocks","test","watchdog","wezterm"]}
 {"id":"ft-2guux","title":"reality-check gaps","description":"Reality-check sweep of the repo (pane 5, session 2026-04-20) against
README.md + AGENTS.md + docs/ft-xbnl0-verification-contract.md. Most
capability claims match the code; the four material gaps below are
worth tracking. No fixes this tick — documentation-only finding.

Methodology: read the README "Supported Surface Matrix" and "Quick
Example", then grep the CLI command tree at `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs`,
the MCP tools at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs`, the web
endpoints at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/router.rs`, and the
runtime layer at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs` +
`runtime_compat.rs`.

Findings (severity ranked):

G1 [MEDIUM] Watch-daemon asupersync claim vs actual reactor dependency
  Evidence:
    - README.md:38-41 "runtime model is asupersync-native",
      "runtime_compat remains as an audited boundary".
    - crates/frankenterm-core/tests/runtime_labruntime.rs:13-18
      explicit comment:
        "Full ObservationRuntime integration tests (startup,
         shutdown, discovery, capture) require the runtime_compat::task
         module to be migrated from tokio::spawn to asupersync::spawn.
         Until that migration completes, ObservationRuntime::start()
         cannot run under asupersync RuntimeFixture because task::spawn
         → tokio::spawn panics without a tokio reactor."
    - runtime.rs:184 uses `crate::runtime_compat::task::spawn_with_cx`
      which still goes through the tokio-backed path when the
      `asupersync-runtime` feature is disabled.
  Why it matters: the primary operator surface (`ft watch`) cannot be
  exercised end-to-end under a pure asupersync runtime fixture today.
  The README is honest that runtime_compat is "an audited boundary";
  the specific gap is that the watch daemon's task spawns transit
  that boundary. Tracked by the ft-xbnl0.2.x epic but worth surfacing
  as a doc clarification on README.md:38-41 ("native with a named
  quarantine").

G2 [MEDIUM] `ft restart` is Unix-only, not disclosed in the support matrix
  Evidence:
    - README.md:124 "Session persistence | Supported with backend
      prerequisite | Snapshots, session inspection, restore, and
      `ft session doctor` are implemented".
    - crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:27032 emits:
        "ft restart is currently supported only on Unix platforms."
      and exits 1 on non-Unix; the full `execute_restart_workflow`
      call path sits inside `#[cfg(unix)]` at line 27036.
  Why it matters: a Windows operator following the README's
  session-persistence story will hit this only at runtime.
  Supportable via an asterisk in the matrix or an explicit
  "Unix-only" caveat in the `ft restart` section.

G3 [LOW] Performance claims lack linked artifacts
  Evidence:
    - README.md:51 "<50ms lag" for delta extraction.
    - README.md:62 "keeps 200+ panes under 1GB vs 4GB+".
    - README.md:113 "TOON reduces token consumption by 40-60%".
    - No matching benchmark artifact path in docs/ referenced from
      these claims; the verification-contract doc at docs/ft-xbnl0-
      verification-contract.md mandates Level C artifact bundles for
      soak/perf-adjacent claims but the README does not cite any.
  Why it matters: performance claims presented as current fact in
  the README should either cite an artifact bundle or be softened to
  "target" / "design goal" until evidence lands. Non-blocking.

G4 [LOW] `SEND_OSC_SEGMENT_LIMIT = 200` silently bounds OSC133 state
  Evidence:
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp.rs:188
        `const SEND_OSC_SEGMENT_LIMIT: usize = 200;`
    - mcp.rs:288-310 `derive_osc_state_from_storage` walks only the
      last 200 segments. If OSC133 markers span further back, the
      derived state is silently incomplete; the helper returns
      `Ok(None)` with no gap marker.
  Why it matters: a quiet-pane edge case (no activity for >200
  segments) would yield an incorrect alt-screen / prompt state via
  the MCP surface without a visible warning. Worth either documenting
  the bound or recording a `markers_unknown_due_to_window_limit`
  signal.

Non-gaps verified present and matching claims:

  - `ft robot state` / `state --include-text`: main.rs:2375-2391
  - `ft robot search --mode {lexical,semantic,hybrid}`:
      main.rs:4633-4711 enum + From impl; semantic/hybrid properly
      gated behind `semantic-search`/`frankensearch` features.
  - `ft mcp serve`: main.rs:33124
  - `ft web` + `/stream/events` + `/stream/deltas`:
      web/router.rs:65,70 — streaming exists; README's
      "more than 'health only'" claim is accurate.
  - `ft distributed agent`: main.rs:165-168 under
      `#[cfg(feature = "distributed")]`.
  - `ft session doctor`, snapshot save/restore/list/inspect/diff:
      SnapshotCommands + SessionCommands enums defined around
      main.rs:4207.
  - `ft tx run`/`tx rollback`/`tx plan`/`tx show`: main.rs:21114+
      via `execute_tx_run_with_executor`.
  - Policy "21 subsystems": unified_telemetry.rs:242 comment; actual
      PolicyEngine struct at policy.rs:3466-3517 declares ~22
      subsystem-shaped fields. Close-enough; no drift worth fixing.
  - tokio/smol usage in supported core: grep finds zero production
      call sites outside `runtime_compat` + `dependency_eradication`
      + `forbidden_dep_guards` (guard files are intentional).

Documented only. No shippable fix this tick per /reality-check-for-project
ground rules. Related: AGENTS.md rule zero (NEVER delete
frankenterm-core) is intact; the four gaps above are additions to the
README, not subtractions from the code.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T03:01:41.726765Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:17:59.915672Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:17:59.913552Z","close_reason":"Reopening-in-error by p5-orch robot-triage pick — the bead was already closed with the four reality-check gaps (G1 runtime_compat quarantine language, G2 ft restart Unix-only caveat, G3 softened perf/token claims, G4 OSC/OSC133 marker window bound) addressed across two commits:

1. 4c3952a9 docs: correct README claims vs actual (ft-2guux) — landed 2026-04-20, covers all four gaps:
   - G1: added 'The remaining watch-daemon/runtime startup path still crosses a named runtime_compat quarantine while the last task-spawn seams are being collapsed' to the Platform Model paragraph
   - G2: appended 'ft restart is currently Unix-only.' to the Session persistence row of the support matrix

 {"id":"ft-2h5wv","title":"Refactor MuxPool (mux_pool.rs) to asupersync","description":"# Refactor MuxPool (mux_pool.rs) to asupersync

## Background
mux_pool.rs (583 LOC) wraps generic Pool for DirectMuxClient connections with WezTerm-specific health checks.

## Dependencies
- Pool refactoring (wa-36idf) must be done first
- DirectMuxClient refactoring (wa-p48pw) must be done first

## Migration
1. Thread Cx through to Pool methods
2. Update health check (list_panes()) to use Cx
3. Update connection factory (DirectMuxClient::connect) to use Cx
4. Ensure pool config works with Budget model

## Comprehensive unit tests (LabRuntime)
1. **Basic lifecycle**: create pool → acquire → use → release → shutdown
2. **Health check integration**: mock unhealthy client, verify pool replaces it
3. **Connection factory**: verify DirectMuxClient::connect called with correct Cx
4. **Idle eviction**: connections idle past timeout are evicted (virtual time)
5. **Concurrent acquire**: N tasks acquire simultaneously, max_size respected
6. **Acquire timeout**: full pool, Budget::deadline fires, clean Cancelled outcome
7. **Pool drain on shutdown**: scope exit waits for all connections returned
8. **Stats accuracy**: verify all counters under concurrent load

Each test logs: pool stats at each step, health check results, connection create/destroy events.

## Acceptance criteria
- MuxPool works with asupersync DirectMuxClient and Pool
- Health checks work through Cx
- Idle eviction works with virtual time
- 8+ LabRuntime tests
- Obligation leak oracle passes

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Pool concurrency testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test concurrent MuxPool operations:
  - Multiple tasks acquiring connections while health checks run — verify no stale connections returned
  - Interleaved acquire + release + eviction — verify pool invariants (active + idle <= max_size)
  - Concurrent shutdown while tasks hold connections — verify clean drain without orphaned connections
  - Race between health check failure and acquire — verify correct fallback to new connection creation

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for pool scaling with connection count**: Add `benches/mux_pool_scaling.rs` measuring:
  - Acquire + release cycle latency as a function of pool size (max_size=1,2,4,8,16,32)
  - Health check overhead per acquire operation
  - Pool scaling: throughput (ops/sec) with increasing concurrent task count
  - Idle eviction scan time as a function of pool size
  - Connection factory overhead (DirectMuxClient::connect latency)

## Test and Observability Plan
1. Add comprehensive unit tests covering happy path, boundary/edge cases, and explicit failure/error behavior for logic introduced or changed by this bead.
2. Add deterministic integration and end-to-end scenario scripts that exercise full workflow behavior, including at least one negative/failure scenario and one recovery scenario.
3. Emit detailed structured logs during tests and e2e runs (timestamp, subsystem, correlation_id, scenario_id, inputs, decisions, outcomes, reason_code/error_code).
4. Persist triage artifacts for failed runs (stdout/stderr, structured logs, snapshots/fixtures, and concise failure summaries) so regressions can be root-caused quickly.
5. Ensure test commands and evidence locations are documented in the bead implementation notes and wired into normal validation/CI paths.

## User Impact
1. Specify the concrete operator/agent workflow pain this bead removes (speed, reliability, safety, or clarity).
2. Define measurable user-facing outcomes (for example p95 latency, failure/timeout rate, time-to-recovery, or reduction in manual intervention steps).
3. Define no-regression UX guarantees and explicit rollback/degradation behavior if those outcomes are not met.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Migrate MuxPool async behavior to asupersync while preserving pool correctness, health-check semantics, and acquisition/release guarantees.

3. Require comprehensive validation: unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end scripts for lifecycle, health-check replacement, backpressure behavior, and timeout/cancellation flows.
4. Require structured diagnostics/logging for pool state transitions (acquire/release/replace/evict), health-check outcomes, and latency metrics.

README.md:128 Session Persistence matrix row:

    | Session persistence | Supported with backend prerequisite |
    Snapshots, session inspection, restore, and `ft session doctor`
    are implemented, but live restore still depends on the current
    WezTerm mux interop boundary. \`ft restart\` is currently
    Unix-only. |

Disclosed: `ft restart` Unix-only.
Undisclosed: `ft snapshot restore` has the *same* cfg gate.

# Reality (code)

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:27100` (ft restart — disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft restart is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:39715` (ft snapshot restore — NOT disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft snapshot restore is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

Same gate, same failure mode. README line 128 specifically mentions
`ft restart` as the Unix-only surface but omits `ft snapshot restore`,
which is also mentioned in the *same* matrix row ("Snapshots, session
inspection, **restore**, and `ft session doctor` are implemented").

A Windows operator reading the matrix is told "restore is
implemented" (true, on Unix) without the cross-platform caveat.

# Failure shape

Operator on Windows runs `ft snapshot restore ...` → eprintln hits
→ exit 1. Workaround unknown.

# Recommended disposition

Update README.md:128 to say:

    Snapshots and session inspection are implemented cross-platform;
    `ft restart` and `ft snapshot restore` are currently Unix-only.

One-line doc change. Alternative: fix the cfg gate. The session
persistence code under `#[cfg(unix)]` likely depends on real Unix
signals / fork semantics — porting is non-trivial. Docs-first is
the pragmatic path.

# Related

ft-2guux previously flagged G2 "`ft restart` Unix-only not in
support matrix" and was closed 2026-04-21 with a README.md:128
update. That update added disclosure for `ft restart` but missed
`ft snapshot restore` — this bead is the follow-up.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"pane8","owner":"pane3-readme-fix","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:55.113196Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:55.619147Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:49:57.208098Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","platform","reality-gap"]}

 {"id":"ft-6mxfs","title":"[mor:security-engineering] ws_dir = root.join('.ft','patterns') is not canonicalized before discovery","description":"# Observation

In `load_packs_from_config`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:949):

    let ws_dir = root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns");
    let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&ws_dir)?;

ft-fbee5 (just closed) fixed the case where a symlink INSIDE
`.ft/patterns/` escapes the sandbox. But the parent path is itself
just a join, no canonicalize. A hostile cloned repo can ship:

    .ft/patterns -> /etc

(a symlink at the directory level.) `ws_dir` canonicalizes to
`/etc`, `discover_packs_from_dir(/etc)` iterates there, and the
per-file sandbox check passes because it canonicalizes with root =
`/etc`. The symlink escape is now one level up.

Similar attack surface for `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` if
that ever returns a joined path the caller doesn't canonicalize.

# Question

Should `load_packs_from_config` canonicalize `ws_dir` and verify the
result resolves *inside* `root_path.canonicalize()` before calling
`discover_packs_from_dir`? That closes the parent-level symlink
vector without needing to change `discover_packs_from_dir`'s
contract.

Stretch: is there a case for centralizing this as a
`sandbox_resolve_dir(dir, root)` helper and using it everywhere the
pack/workflow/policy loaders join a workspace-relative path? The
pattern is probably not unique to pack loading.

# Trust boundary

The repo is the attacker; the user's home directory is the victim.
Everything a hostile repo can make `read_to_string` see is a
potential data-exfil primitive. Treat repo-relative path
construction as crossing a trust boundary, and canonicalize at the
boundary crossing, not at the leaf.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:09.320199Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.174562Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305207Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0239fbd6 — added sandbox_resolve_dir helper, routed load_packs_from_config's ws_dir through it with 4 regression tests (missing, inside-root, toplevel-symlink-escape, end-to-end).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","sandboxing","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-6piwt","title":"[P0 BLOCKER] frankenterm-core lib has 61 compile errors under --no-default-features — blocks all --no-default-features test/bench runs","description":"## Symptom
`cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features` fails with 61 errors. Panes 3 and 4 in the swarm both hit this while trying to verify their respective commits.

## Representative error
```
error[E0599]: no method named `record_event_with_cx` found for this struct
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:6157
  help: there is a method `record_event` with a similar name

error[E0599]: no method named `get_text_with_cx` found for struct `std::sync::Arc<(dyn WeztermInterface + 'static)>`
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:3272:43
  note: `wezterm::PaneTextSource` defines an item `get_text_with_cx`, perhaps you need to implement it
```

## Root cause hypothesis
Asupersync migration landed `_with_cx` **callers** in some files without landing `_with_cx` **implementations** on the corresponding traits/structs, specifically under the default feature set (where the legacy impls are gated off but the new impls aren't gated on).

## Scope
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs (record_event_with_cx)
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs (get_text_with_cx + PaneTextSource trait alignment)
- 61 errors total; likely same pattern elsewhere

## Fix direction
1. Run `cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features 2>&1 | grep 'error\[\|error:' > /tmp/errors.txt` to enumerate all 61.
2. Group by method name; for each missing `*_with_cx` method:
   - If the non-cx variant exists, add a default impl or route `with_cx` through it (respects cancel semantics).
   - If neither exists, revert the caller to the non-cx variant with a TODO referencing ft-xbnl0.2 epic.
3. Verify with `cargo check` clean, then `cargo test --lib --no-default-features`.

## Priority
P0 — blocks swarm from running any tests under --no-default-features, which is the recommended lean test surface.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:29:56.725238Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680762Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680537Z","close_reason":"Added no-feature cx compatibility shims and ungated fallback *_with_cx adapters so frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features compiles again","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-6scm7","title":"[gui][i18n] font.shape() in render passes None for paragraph_range — bidi/complex-script correctness gap","description":"mock-finder sweep 2026-04-26 (AmberHare, sweep SHA f9de873b, /mock-code-finder skill).

Production gap: crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/render/mod.rs:835 calls font.shape(...) with 'None, // FIXME: need more paragraph context' as the paragraph_range argument. The FIXME has lived in this branch since the GUI extraction; without paragraph context the harfbuzz / freetype shaping path cannot resolve bidi (right-to-left embedding levels) or run-boundary-sensitive complex script clusters (Arabic medial vs final forms, Devanagari conjuncts, Mongolian, etc.) correctly. Result: visible mis-rendering of RTL and Indic scripts inside frankenterm-gui panes.

Citation: crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/render/mod.rs:835 — within the cluster shaping loop that iterates ShapedCluster items inside the screen-line render path (the call site is the inner font.shape(...) inside the screen_line render machinery; the FIXME is on the line passing the paragraph_range argument).

Suggested fix:
  1. Audit how the upstream wezterm-equivalent render path threads paragraph context (likely a Range<usize> over the logical line bytes, or a reference to a precomputed bidi paragraph) and re-establish that thread.
  2. Plumb a paragraph context through the cluster builder so the shape call receives Some(&paragraph) for the cluster's enclosing logical line.
  3. Add a render-level golden test (insta or similar) covering 1 RTL string ('السلام عليكم') and 1 Indic string ('नमस्ते') — failure mode here is silent mis-shaping, so a screenshot/text-attribute comparison is the only way to lock the fix.

Acceptance: the FIXME line in render/mod.rs:835 no longer passes None; companion render-golden test for at least one RTL + one Indic sample. Severity rationale (P2 not P3): correctness regression for any pane that renders non-Latin script — visible to international users.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"codex-greyfalcon","created_at":"2026-04-26T23:27:46.754993Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-27T18:57:26.194631Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T18:57:26.194395Z","close_reason":"Shipped ab11211d: render shaping now uses paragraph text/ranges for clusters, with RTL+Indic range regression coverage.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
